  3. By the time we reached, the train had left. (ഞങ്ങൾ എത്തിയപ്പോഴേക്കും train പോയിരുന്നു)
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

❌ (A) has given
• 'Has given' = Present Perfect — present relevance ഉള്ള past action-ന്. Main clause 'we met' = Simple Past → present perfect subordinate clause-ൽ use ചെയ്യരുത്. Tense mismatch!
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Has given' = 'had given' sound-alike → confuse ചെയ്യും. 'Has' (present perfect) vs 'had' (past perfect) — 'met' past tense main clause → 'had given' only.

❌ (C) gave
• 'Gave' = Simple Past — two past events both in simple past = sequence unclear. 'We met her after she gave' = OK in casual speech but doesn't clearly show which event came first.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Gave' natural/simple feel → first thought. പക്ഷേ PSC requires precise grammar → earlier event = 'had given' (Past Perfect) ✓.

❌ (D) was given
• 'Was given' = Simple Past Passive — speech was given BY someone. Here SHE is the one giving the speech (active). 'She was given a speech' = someone gave HER a speech = wrong meaning.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Was given' = formal/official feel → speech context → formal feel. പക്ഷേ she = giver of speech (active), not receiver (passive).
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'HAD = HISTORY! The earlier event has MORE history — so use HAD. The later event is just past — use simple past.' — Speech came first (more history = HAD given). Meeting came second (just past = met).
• 'After' signal: 'after she ___ speech' → blank = earlier event → Past Perfect (had given) always.
• PSC exam strategy: 'After/Before' ഉള്ള sentence-ൽ two past events → blank = earlier event = 'had + V3'. Main clause = simple past. See 'met' (simple past) + 'after' → blank = 'had given'. Guaranteed mark!

questionText:
Choose the correct form of the verb to complete the sentence.
The guests admired my paintings but they __________ my writings.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (C) seemed to have disliked

• 'Admired' = Simple Past → main sentence past tense. Second clause also past → 'seemed' (past tense of 'seem') ✓
• 'Seemed to have disliked' = past tense verb (seemed) + perfect infinitive (to have disliked) = the disliking happened BEFORE/DURING the seeming.
• 'To have disliked' = Perfect Infinitive — past completed action expressed through infinitive form.
• Structure: seemed (past main verb) + to have disliked (perfect infinitive showing completed past action) ✓
• Meaning: 'Guests-ന് paintings ഇഷ്ടമായി, പക്ഷേ writings dislike ആയതായി tense felt.'
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 ഓർക്കാനുള്ള formula: seem/appear/tend + to have + V3 = past completed action expressed through infinitive.
• Perfect Infinitive (to have + V3):
  - Use when: the infinitive action happened BEFORE the main verb or is completed
  - 'seemed to have disliked' = disliking happened → then seeming/appearing was observed
  - Signal: Both actions in past, infinitive action completed
• Tense matching with 'seem/appear':
  - Present context: 'seems to dislike' (present ongoing)
  - Present context, past action: 'seems to have disliked' (past action noted now)
  - Past context: 'seemed to dislike' (past ongoing)
  - Past context, completed action: 'seemed to have disliked' ✓ (past completed)
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Perfect Infinitive (to have + V3) = PSC-ൽ tested!
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. She seemed to have forgotten my name. (forgot = completed past action)
  2. He appears to have left already. (left = completed, noticed now)
  3. They seemed to have enjoyed the show. (enjoyed = completed past)
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

❌ (A) seemed to dislike
• 'Seemed to dislike' = past tense + simple infinitive. Simple infinitive = action happening simultaneously with 'seemed', not completed. 'Seemed to dislike' suggests ongoing disliking at that time.
• 'Seemed to have disliked' (C) = completed past disliking. Given context (past completed reaction), perfect infinitive ✓.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Seemed to dislike' = simpler/more natural feel. Both are grammatically possible but perfect infinitive 'seemed to have disliked' = more precise for completed past reaction.

❌ (B) seem to have disliked
• 'Seem' = present tense. Main clause 'admired' = past. Tense mismatch — present 'seem' with past 'admired' = inconsistent narrative tense.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Seem to have disliked' = present + perfect infinitive = also natural-sounding. പക്ഷേ 'admired' (past) → 'seemed' (past) needed for tense consistency.

❌ (D) seem to dislike
• 'Seem to dislike' = present tense + simple infinitive. Double error: (1) 'seem' present tense conflicts with 'admired' past, (2) simple infinitive instead of perfect infinitive for completed action.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Seem to dislike' = simplest option → first instinct. पक्षे two tense errors make this incorrect.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'SEEMED (past) + TO HAVE (perfect) = past story about something already done!' — 'Admired... but seemed to have disliked' = guests already disliked writings (completed feeling) → 'to have disliked' (perfect infinitive) shows this completion.
• Quick test: Is the main verb past tense? → Use 'seemed'. Is the infinitive action completed/done before? → Use 'to have + V3'. Both yes = 'seemed to have disliked' ✓.
• PSC exam strategy: Seem/appear + infinitive questions → Step 1: Check main clause tense (seemed/seems). Step 2: Check if infinitive action completed → 'to have + V3'. Step 3: 'Admired' = past → 'seemed' (not 'seem'). Action completed → 'to have disliked'. = (C) ✓. Guaranteed mark!

questionText: Identify the correct sentence.
optionA: I think him to be a silly boy
optionB: I think him as a silly boy
optionC: I think him being a silly boy
optionD: I think him a silly boy

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (D) I think him a silly boy

• 'Think + object + complement' = correct structure → 'I think him a silly boy' ✓
• Pattern: 'Think + object pronoun (him/her/them) + noun/adjective complement'
• No preposition, no infinitive, no gerund needed — direct complement structure.
• 'I think him a silly boy' = 'I consider him to be a silly boy' meaning.
• This is a factitive verb construction — 'think' here = 'consider/regard as'.
• Meaning: 'ഞാൻ അവനെ ഒരു മണ്ടൻ ആൺകുട്ടി ആണെന്ന് കരുതുന്നു.'
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 ഓർക്കാനുള്ള formula: THINK/CONSIDER/FIND/CALL + object + complement (NO 'as', NO 'to be', NO 'being')!
• Factitive verbs — 'think' used as 'consider':
  - think + object + noun/adjective = ✓ (direct complement)
  - think + object + 'as' + noun = ✗ (no 'as' with think)
  - think + object + 'to be' + noun = old-fashioned but sometimes accepted
  - think + object + 'being' + noun = ✗ (gerund not used here)
• Other factitive verbs follow same pattern:
  - call: 'They call him a genius.' ✓
  - consider: 'We consider her a friend.' ✓
  - find: 'I find this job difficult.' ✓
  - name: 'They named the baby Joy.' ✓
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Factitive verb structure — 'think/consider + object + complement' PSC-ൽ tested!
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. I consider him a good doctor. (not 'consider him as')
  2. She finds him charming. (not 'finds him as charming')
  3. They call her a genius. (not 'call her as')
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

❌ (A) I think him to be a silly boy
• 'Think + object + to be' = grammatically dated/formal but in modern English, 'think' as factitive verb prefers direct complement without 'to be'.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'To be' = formal/correct-sounding → 'think him TO BE' feels more complete. But modern English grammar: 'think him a silly boy' (no 'to be') = preferred form ✓.

❌ (B) I think him as a silly boy
• 'As' = used with 'regard/describe/treat/see', NOT with 'think'. 'Think + object + as' = incorrect collocation.
• Correct: 'I regard him AS a silly boy' ✓ / 'I see him AS a silly boy' ✓ / 'I think him a silly boy' ✓
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'As' = comparison/description feel → 'think him as' natural feel. But 'think + as' = wrong collocation. 'Think + object + complement' (no 'as') ✓.

❌ (C) I think him being a silly boy
• 'Being' = gerund/present participle — 'think + object + being + noun' = wrong structure. Gerund-complement not used with factitive 'think'.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Being' = continuous/descriptive feel → 'him being silly' = description feel. But 'think + being' = not standard construction. Direct complement needed.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'THINK HIM A FOOL — no middle words! Think takes object + complement DIRECTLY — no 'as', no 'to be', no 'being' in between!' — Clean and direct: 'I think him a silly boy' = object (him) + complement (a silly boy) directly.
• Quick formula for factitive verbs: THINK/CONSIDER/FIND/CALL + [object] + [noun/adjective] — no extra words!
• PSC exam strategy: 'Think/consider/find/call' sentence → eliminate options with 'as', 'being'. 'To be' = old-fashioned, direct complement = modern. 'I think him a silly boy' = clean structure ✓. Guaranteed mark!

questionText:
Choose the correct meaning of the term.
Ornithology means

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (A) Study of birds

• 'Ornithology' = 'പക്ഷി ശാസ്ത്രം' = the scientific study of birds.
• Word root: Greek 'ornis/ornithos' = bird + 'logos/logy' = study/science.
• Ornithologist = പക്ഷികളെ study ചെയ്യുന്ന scientist.
• Famous ornithologists: Salim Ali = India's 'Birdman' — India's most famous ornithologist!
• Kerala connection: Kerala = bird-watchers' paradise. Ornithology = very relevant study here.
• Meaning: 'Ornithology = പക്ഷികളുടെ ശാസ്ത്രീയ പഠനം — habits, migration, species, breeding എല്ലാം.'
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 ഓർക്കാനുള്ള formula: Greek root 'ornithos' = bird + 'logy' = study → Ornithology = Study of birds!
• '-logy' suffix = science/study of. PSC-ൽ ഈ suffix ഉള്ള words frequent:
  - Ornithology → birds (ornithos = bird)
  - Zoology → animals (zoo = animal)
  - Botany/Phytology → plants (phyto = plant)
  - Geology → earth/rocks (geo = earth)
  - Osteology → bones (osteo = bone)
  - Herpetology → reptiles (herp = crawling thing)
  - Entomology → insects (entomo = insect)
  - Ichthyology → fish (ichthys = fish)
  - Anthropology → humans (anthropo = human)
  - Psychology → mind (psycho = mind)
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: '-logy' words = PSC-ൽ highly frequent! Root word memorize ചെയ്‌ത് GO!
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. Ornithology is the scientific study of birds. (birds = ornithology)
  2. The ornithologist studied migratory birds. (bird scientist)
  3. Salim Ali was a famous ornithologist. (bird expert)
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

❌ (B) Study of bones
• Study of bones = 'Osteology' (Greek 'osteo' = bone). Ornithology ≠ Osteology.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Both 'Ornithology' and 'Osteology' start with 'O' → confusion! 'Osteo' (bones) ≠ 'Ornitho' (birds). Remember: 'Ornith-' sounds like bird sounds (tweeting!) → birds.

❌ (C) Study of reptiles
• Study of reptiles = 'Herpetology' (Greek 'herp' = creeping/crawling). Ornithology ≠ Herpetology.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Reptiles = another animal group → could feel like 'Ornithology'. But 'Herp' = reptiles, 'Ornitho' = birds. Completely different roots.

❌ (D) Study of rocks
• Study of rocks = 'Petrology' or 'Geology' (Greek 'petros' = rock, 'geo' = earth). Ornithology ≠ Geology.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Rocks' and 'birds' both found in nature → nature study confusion. But '-logy' word root decides the answer: 'Ornitho' = bird (not rock).
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'ORNITHO sounds like a BIRD calling — OR-NI-THO! (like a bird tweet sound!) → BIRDS!' — When you hear 'Ornithology', imagine a bird tweeting 'ORNITHOOOO!' → Study of BIRDS!
• Must-know '-logy' list for PSC:
  - Ornithology = Birds 🐦
  - Osteology = Bones 🦴
  - Herpetology = Reptiles 🦎
  - Entomology = Insects 🐛
  - Ichthyology = Fish 🐟
  - Geology = Earth/Rocks 🪨
• PSC exam strategy: '-logy' word question → identify the root BEFORE '-logy'. 'Ornitho' = bird root → Study of birds = (A). Options B, C, D have different roots (osteo/herp/geo). Guaranteed mark!

questionText:
Choose the word which best expresses the meaning of the given word.
Distasteful

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (B) unpleasant

• 'Distasteful' = 'അരോചകം, അറപ്പ് ഉണ്ടാക്കുന്ന, ഇഷ്ടമില്ലാത്ത' = causing dislike or disgust.
• 'Unpleasant' = 'അസുഖകരം, ഇഷ്ടപ്പെടാത്ത' — not pleasant, causing discomfort.
• 'Distasteful' = strongly synonymous with 'unpleasant' — both = not agreeable/likable.
• Root analysis: 'Dis-' (negative prefix) + 'taste' (liking/preference) + '-ful' (full of) = full of dislike ✓
• Meaning: 'Distasteful = ഇഷ്ടമില്ലാത്ത, rude/offensive behavior or experience'.
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 ഓർക്കാനുള്ള formula: 'DIS-' prefix = negative/opposite. 'TASTE' = liking/preference. '-FUL' = full of. So 'DISTASTEFUL' = full of un-liking = UNPLEASANT!
• Word analysis of 'distasteful':
  - Prefix: 'dis-' = not, opposite of (disagree, dislike, disorder)
  - Root: 'taste' = liking, preference (not just food taste)
  - Suffix: '-ful' = having the quality of
  - Combined: having the quality of disliking = unpleasant, disagreeable
• Word family:
  - Distasteful (adjective) = disagreeable, unpleasant
  - Distaste (noun) = dislike, aversion
  - Distastefully (adverb) = in an unpleasant manner
• Synonyms chain: distasteful = unpleasant = disagreeable = offensive = repugnant = objectionable
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: 'Dis-' prefix words + synonyms = PSC frequent combination!
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. He found the task distasteful. (task-ൽ dislike ഉണ്ടായി)
  2. Her behaviour was distasteful to everyone. (everyone-ന് unpleasant ആയി)
  3. The movie had some distasteful scenes. (offensive/unpleasant scenes)
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

❌ (A) flavoured
• 'Flavoured' = a particular taste (chocolate-flavoured, lemon-flavoured). Literal food meaning.
• 'Distasteful' root 'taste' ≠ food taste. 'Dis-taste-ful' = figurative disliking, not food flavour.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Dis-TASTE-ful' → 'taste' → food → 'flavoured' = food related → logical trap! But 'taste' in 'distasteful' = liking/preference (figurative), not food taste (literal).

❌ (C) tasteful
• 'Tasteful' = showing good taste, elegant, stylish — OPPOSITE of distasteful!
• 'Distaste-ful' vs 'Taste-ful' = antonyms (opposites), not synonyms!
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Distasteful' contains 'tasteful' → looks related! But 'dis-' prefix = negative → 'distasteful' = NOT tasteful. 'Tasteful' is the ANTONYM, not synonym.

❌ (D) satisfying
• 'Satisfying' = giving satisfaction, fulfilling, pleasant — OPPOSITE of distasteful!
• 'Distasteful' = unpleasant ≠ 'Satisfying' = pleasant. Complete opposite meaning.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Satisfying' = positive word → pleasant feeling → could feel related to 'taste'. But 'dis-tasteful' = negative (unpleasant), not positive (satisfying).
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'DIS-TASTE-FUL = DISliked, not TASTEFUL! Dis = NOT. Tasteful = good. So Distasteful = NOT good = UNPLEASANT!' — When you see 'DIS-' at start → flip to negative meaning! Tasteful = good → Distasteful = NOT good = unpleasant.
• 'Dis-' prefix words PSC list:
  - Distasteful = unpleasant
  - Disinterested = impartial (not 'uninterested'!)
  - Discreet = careful/tactful
  - Dismal = dreary/depressing
  - Dispel = drive away
• PSC exam strategy: Synonym question → Eliminate ANTONYMS first. 'Tasteful' (C) = antonym → eliminate. 'Satisfying' (D) = positive → opposite → eliminate. 'Flavoured' (A) = literal food meaning → wrong context → eliminate. 'Unpleasant' (B) = negative = matches 'distasteful' = ✓. Guaranteed mark!

questionText: Each of the employees _________ working hard.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (B) is

• 'Each of the employees' — ഇവിടെ subject ആരാണ്? 'Each' = singular pronoun.
• 'Each of + plural noun' structure-ൽ verb = singular ആകണം — 'Each' determines the verb, not 'employees'.
• 'Each' = ഓരോരുത്തരും individually refer ചെയ്യുന്നതിനാൽ singular → 'is' ✓
• 'Employees' plural feel കൊടുത്താലും — 'each of' = singular marker.
• Meaning: 'ജീവനക്കാർ ഓരോരുത്തരും കഠിനമായി ജോലി ചെയ്യുകയാണ്.'
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 ഓർക്കാനുള്ള formula: EACH / EVERY / EITHER / NEITHER + of + plural noun → SINGULAR verb!
• 'Each of' rule:
  - Each of the students IS present. ✓ (not 'are')
  - Each of the books HAS a cover. ✓ (not 'have')
  - Each of them IS responsible. ✓
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: 'Each/Every/Either/Neither + of + plural' = singular verb — PSC-ൽ ഏറ്റവും repeat ആകുന്ന trap!
• ഓർക്കേണ്ട distributive pronouns (singular verb take ചെയ്യുന്നവ):
  - each, every, either, neither → always singular
  - nobody, everyone, someone, anyone → always singular
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. Each of the boys is playing. (boys plural, but 'each' = singular → is)
  2. Every employee is required to attend. (every → singular → is)
  3. Neither of the options is correct. (neither → singular → is)
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

❌ (A) are
• 'Are' = plural verb. 'Employees' plural ആണ്, പക്ഷേ subject = 'Each' (singular).
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Employees' = plural → 'are' natural feel. But 'Each of the employees' → 'Each' = subject → singular → 'is'.

❌ (C) be
• 'Be' = base form — present tense statement-ൽ 'be' ഉപയോഗിക്കില്ല (only in subjunctive/imperative).
• 'Each is working' = present continuous → auxiliary 'is' needed, not 'be'.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Be' = neutral base form feel → confusion. But statement sentence-ൽ 'be' alone = always wrong.

❌ (D) none
• 'None' as a verb — grammatically impossible. 'None' = pronoun, not a verb.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'None' = 'not any' feel → confuse ചെയ്യും. But verb position-ൽ 'none' use ചെയ്യാനേ കഴിയില്ല.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'EACH = ONE! Each means one at a time — ONE person = singular verb IS!' — 'Each of the employees' = ഓരോ employee-ഉം separately = ONE = 'is'.
• Quick rule: 'Each/Every/Either/Neither' കണ്ടാൽ → singular verb (is/has/was) choose ചെയ്യുക. 'Are/have/were' = eliminate.
• PSC exam strategy: 'Each of the ___' pattern ഉള്ള question → 'is' option ഉടൻ mark ചെയ്യുക. 'Are' = always wrong here. Guaranteed mark!

questionText: She accused me __________ talking loudly.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (A) of

• 'Accuse someone OF something' = English-ലെ fixed collocation — 'of' മാത്രം ✓
• 'She accused me of talking loudly' = 'ഉറക്കെ സംസാരിച്ചു' എന്ന് അവൾ എന്നെ കുറ്റപ്പെടുത്തി.
• Pattern: accuse + object + OF + gerund (V-ing) ✓
• 'Accuse of' = standard legal/everyday English collocation — never 'accuse for/about/after'.
• Meaning: 'ഉച്ചത്തിൽ സംസാരിച്ചതിന് അവൾ എന്നെ കുറ്റപ്പെടുത്തി.'
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 ഓർക്കാനുള്ള formula: ACCUSE + person + OF + V-ing (gerund) — always!
• 'OF' ഉപയോഗിക്കുന്ന verb collocations (PSC must-know):
  - accuse OF → കുറ്റം ആരോപിക്കുക
  - suspect OF → സംശയിക്കുക
  - convict OF → ശിക്ഷിക്കുക
  - guilty OF → കുറ്റക്കാരൻ
  - capable OF → കഴിവുള്ള
  - fond OF → ഇഷ്ടമുള്ള
  - afraid OF → ഭയമുള്ള
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: verb + preposition collocations = PSC-ൽ highly frequent!
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. He was accused of stealing. (മോഷ്ടിച്ചതിന് കുറ്റാരോപണം)
  2. She is suspected of lying. (നുണ പറഞ്ഞതിന് സംശയം)
  3. They convicted him of murder. (കൊലപാതകത്തിന് ശിക്ഷ)
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

❌ (B) for
• 'Accuse for' = wrong collocation. 'Blame FOR' = correct (blame for = കുറ്റം ചുമത്തുക), but 'accuse' = 'OF' only.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'For' = reason indicate ചെയ്യും → 'accused for talking' = reason feel. But 'accuse' = 'of' fixed pair. 'Blame for' ≠ 'accuse of' — different verbs, different prepositions!

❌ (C) about
• 'Accuse about' = not a standard English collocation. 'About' = topic indicate ചെയ്യും, not accusation.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'About talking' = talking-നെ കുറിച്ച് feel → 'about' natural sound. But 'accuse' = 'of' fixed. 'Complain about' ✓ / 'accuse of' ✓ — confuse ചെയ്യരുത്.

❌ (D) after
• 'Accuse after' = grammatically impossible meaning. 'After' = time/sequence indicate ചെയ്യും — accusation context-ൽ ഉപയോഗിക്കില്ല.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'After talking' = sequence feel → she accused me after I talked. But 'accuse + after' = not a collocation.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'ACCUSED OF a CRIME — OF always follows accusation! Like in court: accused OF theft, OF murder, OF lying!' — Legal language: 'accused OF' = always OF. ഒരിക്കലും 'accused FOR/ABOUT/AFTER' ഉപയോഗിക്കില്ല.
• Quick list: accuse OF, suspect OF, convict OF, guilty OF — all crimes/wrongdoings use 'OF'.
• PSC exam strategy: 'Accuse + blank' question → 'of' option ഉടൻ choose ചെയ്യുക. 'for/about/after' = all eliminate. Guaranteed mark!

questionText: We have been living here __________ five years.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (C) for

• 'Five years' = a period/duration of time (കാലദൈർഘ്യം) → 'for' ✓
• 'For' = duration indicate ചെയ്യുന്ന preposition — 'for + number + years/months/days'
• 'We have been living here for five years' = 'ഞങ്ങൾ അഞ്ച് വർഷമായി ഇവിടെ താമസിക്കുന്നു.'
• Present Perfect Continuous (have been living) + for = duration ✓
• Key distinction: 'for' = duration (5 years, 3 months, 2 hours). 'since' = starting point (2019, Monday, morning).
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 ഓർക്കാനുള്ള formula: FOR + DURATION (period of time). SINCE + POINT IN TIME.
• FOR vs SINCE (PSC most-tested distinction):
  - FOR + duration: for five years, for two hours, for a week, for ages
  - SINCE + point: since 2019, since Monday, since morning, since childhood
• Test: 'five years' = duration (കാലദൈർഘ്യം) → FOR ✓
• 'Five years' = not a specific starting point → SINCE ✗
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: FOR vs SINCE = PSC-ൽ ഏറ്റവും frequently tested preposition pair!
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. I have been waiting FOR two hours. (two hours = duration)
  2. She has lived here SINCE 2015. (2015 = starting point)
  3. He has been working FOR three years. (three years = duration)
  4. They have known each other SINCE childhood. (childhood = starting point)
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

❌ (A) since
• 'Since' = point in time (specific moment) indicate ചെയ്യുന്നു. 'Five years' = duration, not a point.
• 'Since five years' = wrong. 'Since 2019' ✓ / 'for five years' ✓
• 🪤 PSC Trap: ഏറ്റവും common mistake! Both 'since' and 'for' = Perfect tense-ൽ ഉപയോഗിക്കും. But 'five years' = duration → 'for'. 'Since 2019' ✓ but 'since five years' ✗.

❌ (B) before
• 'Before' = earlier time indicate ചെയ്യുന്നു. 'We have been living before five years' = meaningless/wrong.
• 'Before' = sequence/earlier time preposition, not duration marker.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Before' sounds like time context → time feel. But 'before' = earlier than, not duration.

❌ (D) about
• 'About' = approximately (ഏകദേശം). 'For about five years' = acceptable informally, but 'for' alone is the correct answer here since 'about' is approximate.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'About five years' = 'approximately five years' casual feel. But 'for' = grammatically precise answer for duration.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'FOR = a span of time you can MEASURE! SINCE = a specific moment you can POINT TO on a calendar!' — FOR five years (measure/count), SINCE 2019 (point on calendar). 'Five years' = measurable span → FOR!
• Quick test: Ask 'how long?' → FOR. Ask 'from when?' → SINCE.
• PSC exam strategy: 'Number + years/months/days/hours' → always FOR. Specific year/day/season → always SINCE. 'Five years' = number + years → FOR. Guaranteed mark!

questionText: Seena __________ tomorrow to attend the wedding.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (D) will come

• 'Tomorrow' = ഭാവി (future) signal word → future tense ✓
• 'Will come' = Simple Future tense — future planned action ✓
• 'Seena will come tomorrow to attend the wedding' = 'Seena നാളെ വിവാഹത്തിൽ പങ്കെടുക്കാൻ വരും.'
• 'Tomorrow' കണ്ടാൽ → Simple Future (will + V1) ✓
• Pattern: Subject + will + base verb (V1) = future action
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 ഓർക്കാനുള്ള formula: TOMORROW / NEXT WEEK / SOON / LATER → WILL + V1 (Simple Future)
• Future tense signal words:
  - tomorrow, next week, next year, next month
  - soon, later, in the future, shortly
  - tonight (future context), the day after tomorrow
• Future tense structure: Subject + WILL + V1 (base form)
  - Seena will come (not 'will comes' — base form after 'will'!)
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Signal words + tense matching = PSC-ൽ ഇടയ്ക്കിടെ!
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. He will arrive tomorrow. (tomorrow = future)
  2. They will meet next week. (next week = future)
  3. She will call soon. (soon = future)
  4. I will finish it later. (later = future)
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

❌ (A) come
• 'Come' = Simple Present base form. Present tense-ൽ 'Seena come' = wrong (subject-verb agreement error too — 'Seena comes').
• 'Tomorrow' = future → present tense ഉപയോഗിക്കാൻ കഴിയില്ല here.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Come' = simple/base form → familiar. But 'tomorrow' = future → 'will come' needed.

❌ (B) has come
• 'Has come' = Present Perfect tense — past action with present relevance. 'Tomorrow' = future → past tense impossible.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Has come' = correct-sounding formal phrase. But Present Perfect = past-to-present; 'tomorrow' = future. Tense mismatch!

❌ (C) coming
• 'Coming' = present participle (gerund/continuous form). Without auxiliary 'is', 'coming' alone as main verb = incomplete sentence.
• 'Seena coming tomorrow' = incomplete (no auxiliary). 'Seena IS coming tomorrow' ✓ — but 'coming' alone = wrong.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Coming tomorrow' = conversational feel. But without 'is', 'coming' alone = grammatically incomplete.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'TOMORROW = WILL! See tomorrow, write will — it's a deal!' — 'Tomorrow' = future → 'will' = future helper. No exceptions! 'Tomorrow' + 'will' = always partners.
• Signal word → tense:
  - yesterday → Simple Past (V2)
  - now/currently → Present Continuous (is/are + V-ing)
  - already/just → Present Perfect (has/have + V3)
  - tomorrow/next → Simple Future (will + V1)
• PSC exam strategy: 'Tomorrow' ഉള്ള sentence → 'will + V1' option choose ചെയ്യുക. Guaranteed mark!

questionText: It was __________ unanimous decision.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (A) a

• 'Unanimous' = ഉച്ചാരണം 'you-NAN-i-mous' — ആദ്യ sound = /j/ (consonant sound).
• Article rule: a/an = SOUND-based, not spelling-based! 'U' letter ആണ്, പക്ഷേ sound = /j/ (consonant) → 'a' ✓
• 'A unanimous decision' = 'ഒരു ഏകോപിത തീരുമാനം' ✓
• 'An' = vowel SOUND ആദ്യം വരുമ്പോൾ. 'Unanimous' vowel letter (U) ആണെങ്കിലും consonant sound → 'a'.
• Meaning: 'അത് ഒരു ഏകകണ്ഠമായ തീരുമാനം ആയിരുന്നു.'
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 ഓർക്കാനുള്ള formula: A/AN = SOUND, not SPELLING! Vowel SOUND → AN. Consonant SOUND → A.
• Tricky 'U' words — consonant sound /j/ → 'A' (not 'an'):
  - a unanimous decision (you-NAN-i-mous)
  - a university (you-ni-VER-si-ty)
  - a unique opportunity (you-NEEK)
  - a useful tool (you-SE-ful)
  - a union (YOU-nion)
• Tricky words — vowel sound → 'AN' (even if consonant letter):
  - an hour (h silent → 'our')
  - an honest man (h silent → 'on-est')
  - an MBA (em-bee-ay — vowel sound 'e')
  - an X-ray (ex-ray — vowel sound 'e')
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: 'Unanimous/university/unique/useful' article trap = PSC-ൽ classic!
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. It was a unique experience. (you-NEEK = consonant /j/)
  2. She is a university student. (you-ni = consonant /j/)
  3. It was an unanimous... ✗ / a unanimous ✓
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

❌ (B) an
• ഏറ്റവും common mistake! 'Unanimous' 'U' vowel letter-ൽ തുടങ്ങുന്നു → 'an' feel.
• But 'U' in unanimous = /j/ consonant sound 'you' → 'a' not 'an'.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: U = vowel letter → 'an unanimous' = natural sounding. But SOUND rule: unanimous = 'you-nanimous' → consonant → 'a'! Classic PSC trap.

❌ (C) none
• 'Unanimous decision' = countable singular noun → article needed. 'None' (no article) = wrong for singular countable nouns in this context.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'None' = skip article feel → clean sentence feel. But singular countable noun 'decision' → article required.

❌ (D) the
• 'The' = definite article (specific/known item). 'A unanimous decision' = first mention, one of many possible decisions → indefinite 'a' needed.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'The decision' = sounds specific/important → 'the' feel. But no specific decision established → 'a' (indefinite).
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'U words starting with YOU sound = A, not AN! A University, A Unanimous, A Unique, A Useful, A Union — all start with YOU sound!' — Say it aloud: 'you-nanimous' → starts with Y sound → 'A'.
• Opposite trap: 'An Hour, An Honest, An MBA, An X-ray' — letter H/M/X but vowel sound → 'an'.
• PSC exam strategy: 'Unanimous/university/unique/useful/union' question → always 'a' (not 'an'). Say the word aloud and listen to the first sound. Consonant sound = 'a'. Guaranteed mark!

questionText: One who treats skin diseases is known as __________.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (D) Dermatologist

• 'Dermatologist' = ചർമ്മ രോഗ വിദഗ്ധൻ — skin diseases treat ചെയ്യുന്ന doctor.
• Root: Greek 'derma' = skin (ചർമ്മം) + 'logos' = study + 'ist' = specialist.
• Dermatology = ചർമ്മ ശാസ്ത്രം; Dermatologist = ചർമ്മ രോഗ ഡോക്ടർ.
• Treats: acne, eczema, psoriasis, skin allergies, rashes — all skin conditions.
• Meaning: 'ചർമ്മ രോഗങ്ങൾ ചികിത്സിക്കുന്ന വ്യക്തിയെ Dermatologist എന്ന് വിളിക്കുന്നു.'
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 ഓർക്കാനുള്ള formula: DERMA (Greek) = SKIN → DERMATOLOGIST = skin specialist!
• Medical specialists — root word memory:
  - Dermatologist → 'derma' = skin (ചർമ്മം)
  - Psychologist → 'psycho' = mind (മനസ്സ്)
  - Ophthalmologist → 'ophthalmos' = eye (കണ്ണ്)
  - Gynaecologist → 'gyne' = woman (സ്ത്രീ) — female diseases
  - Cardiologist → 'cardio' = heart (ഹൃദയം)
  - Neurologist → 'neuro' = nerve (നാഡി)
  - Orthopaedic → 'ortho' = bone/straight (അസ്ഥി)
  - Paediatrician → 'paed' = child (കുട്ടി)
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Medical specialists one-word substitutes = PSC-ൽ highly frequent!
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. She visited a dermatologist for her skin allergy. (ചർമ്മ അലർജിക്ക്)
  2. The ophthalmologist checked her vision. (കണ്ണ് specialist)
  3. A cardiologist treats heart problems. (ഹൃദയ specialist)
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

❌ (A) Psychologist
• 'Psychologist' = മനഃശാസ്ത്രജ്ഞൻ — mind/behaviour treat ചെയ്യുന്നവൻ (not skin).
• 'Psycho' = mind → Psychologist = mental health specialist.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: '-ologist' suffix common → all medical specialist feel. 'Psycho' = mind, not skin. ഓർക്കുക: ചർമ്മം = 'derma'.

❌ (B) Ophthalmologist
• 'Ophthalmologist' = നേത്ര വൈദ്യൻ — eye specialist. 'Ophthalmos' = eye.
• Treats: vision problems, glaucoma, cataracts — eye diseases, not skin.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Long complicated word → important/specialist feel. 'Ophthalm' = eye root — skin ഇല്ല.

❌ (C) Gynaecologist
• 'Gynaecologist' = സ്ത്രീ രോഗ വിദഗ്ധ — female reproductive system specialist. 'Gyne' = woman.
• Treats: women's health issues — pregnancy, menstrual disorders, not skin diseases.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Medical doctor feel → could be any specialist. 'Gyne' = woman, not skin.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'DERMA sounds like DERM = SKIN in Malayalam too! DERMatologist = SKIN doctor! Think of dermal cream for skin!' — Dermal cream = skin cream → Dermatologist = skin doctor!
• Must-know doctor list for PSC:
  - Skin → Dermatologist 🧴
  - Eye → Ophthalmologist 👁️
  - Mind → Psychologist 🧠
  - Heart → Cardiologist ❤️
  - Child → Paediatrician 👶
  - Woman → Gynaecologist 👩
  - Bone → Orthopaedician 🦴
• PSC exam strategy: 'Skin diseases' → Dermatologist. Root 'derma' = skin = always. Guaranteed mark!

questionText: Do you mind __________ the answers?

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (A) writing

• 'Mind' = gerund (V-ing) take ചെയ്യുന്ന verb — fixed rule!
• 'Do you mind writing the answers?' = 'ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ എഴുതാൻ നിങ്ങൾക്ക് ഇഷ്ടമില്ലേ / mind ആണോ?'
• Pattern: mind + V-ing (gerund) — 'to write' അല്ല, 'writing' ✓
• 'Mind' = dislike/object to — always followed by gerund, never infinitive.
• Meaning: 'ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ എഴുതുന്നതിൽ നിങ്ങൾക്ക് തടസ്സമുണ്ടോ?'
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 ഓർക്കാനുള്ള formula: MIND + V-ING (gerund) — always! Never 'mind to + V1'.
• Verbs always followed by GERUND (V-ing) — PSC must-know list:
  - mind + V-ing → 'mind writing'
  - enjoy + V-ing → 'enjoy reading'
  - avoid + V-ing → 'avoid going'
  - finish + V-ing → 'finish eating'
  - consider + V-ing → 'consider moving'
  - suggest + V-ing → 'suggest trying'
  - keep + V-ing → 'keep working'
  - risk + V-ing → 'risk losing'
  - deny + V-ing → 'deny stealing'
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Gerund vs Infinitive after specific verbs = PSC-ൽ frequent!
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. Do you mind waiting? (wait → waiting ✓)
  2. He enjoys playing cricket. (play → playing ✓)
  3. She avoided going there. (go → going ✓)
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

❌ (B) to write
• 'To write' = infinitive. 'Mind + to write' = wrong — 'mind' never takes infinitive.
• 'Mind to write' = incorrect English. Mind takes ONLY gerund.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'To write' = formal/correct-sounding. Many verbs DO take infinitive (want to, plan to, hope to). But 'mind' is NOT one of them. 'Mind + V-ing' only.

❌ (C) write
• 'Write' = bare infinitive (base form). After 'mind', base form without 'to' = also wrong.
• Only gerund ('writing') works after 'mind'.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Write' = simplest/shortest option → first instinct. But 'mind' requires '-ing' form, not base form.

❌ (D) wrote
• 'Wrote' = Simple Past tense. Tense ഇവിടെ relevant ഇല്ല — verb form (gerund) question ആണ്.
• 'Do you mind wrote?' = tense + structure = both wrong.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Wrote' = past writing context feel. But this is a present question about willingness, not past action.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'MIND the ING! Do you MIND? Always add -ING after MIND!' — 'Mind' = -ing partner. mind + writing, mind + waiting, mind + coming — always -ing.
• Gerund verb list song: 'Enjoy, avoid, mind, finish, keep, suggest — all take -ING, never forget!'
• PSC exam strategy: 'Mind + blank' → choose the '-ing' option immediately. 'To write/write/wrote' = all eliminate. 'Writing' = only correct form. Guaranteed mark!

questionText: What is the meaning of the word 'dormant'?

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (D) inactive

• 'Dormant' = 'നിഷ്ക്രിയം, ഉറക്കത്തിലുള്ള, temporarily inactive' — sleeping but not dead.
• 'Inactive' = 'പ്രവർത്തനരഹിതം' — not active/working = synonym of dormant ✓
• Word root: Latin 'dormire' = to sleep (ഉറങ്ങുക) → dormant = sleeping state.
• Real-world examples: 'dormant volcano' (ഉറക്കത്തിലുള്ള അഗ്നിപർവ്വതം), 'dormant account' (നിഷ്ക്രിയ account).
• Meaning: 'Dormant = ഒരു കാര്യം temporarily നിർത്തിവച്ച/ഉറങ്ങിക്കിടക്കുന്ന അവസ്ഥ.'
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 ഓർക്കാനുള്ള formula: DORMANT = DORMIR (sleep in Latin/French) → sleeping/inactive state!
• Word family of 'dormant':
  - dormant (adjective) = inactive, sleeping
  - dormancy (noun) = the state of being dormant
  - dormitory (noun) = sleeping room (dorm = dormir = sleep)
• Synonyms of dormant: inactive, sleeping, latent, quiescent, resting, suspended
• Antonyms of dormant: active, awake, working, functioning, operational
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Vocabulary synonyms — '-ant' adjectives from Latin roots = PSC frequent!
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. The volcano has been dormant for centuries. (ഉറക്കത്തിൽ, not erupting)
  2. His talent lay dormant for years. (hidden/inactive talent)
  3. A dormant bank account will be frozen. (inactive account)
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

❌ (A) active
• 'Active' = 'സക്രിയം, ഊർജ്ജസ്വലം' — dormant-ന്റെ ANTONYM (opposite), synonym ഇല്ല!
• 'Dormant volcano' = NOT active. 'Active volcano' = erupting/alive.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Active' = familiar positive word. But question = meaning of 'dormant' → 'active' = exact opposite!

❌ (B) working
• 'Working' = 'പ്രവർത്തിക്കുന്ന' — also opposite of dormant. Dormant = NOT working.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Working' = common word → easy answer feel. But dormant = not working, so 'working' = antonym.

❌ (C) smart
• 'Smart' = 'ബുദ്ധിശാലി, clever' — dormant-ഉമായി ഒരു relation-ഉം ഇല്ല.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Smart' = positive/impressive word → could feel like a compliment meaning. But dormant has nothing to do with intelligence.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'DORMANT = DORMITORY = sleeping room! Dormant = sleeping/inactive! Things in a DORM are sleeping!' — Dormitory (hostel sleeping room) → DORM → sleep → dormant = sleeping/inactive state.
• Dormant = things that COULD be active but are currently sleeping:
  - Dormant volcano 🌋 (sleeping, could erupt)
  - Dormant seed 🌱 (not yet sprouted)
  - Dormant account 🏦 (not being used)
• PSC exam strategy: 'Dormant' = inactive. See 'active/working' → eliminate (opposites). See 'smart' → eliminate (unrelated). 'Inactive' = ✓. Guaranteed mark!

questionText: The evening was __________ than the morning.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (B) hotter

• 'Than' = comparative degree signal word → comparative form ആവശ്യം.
• 'Hot' = one-syllable adjective → comparative = '-er' suffix add ചെയ്യുക: hot → hotter ✓
• 'The evening was hotter than the morning' = 'morning-ഇനേക്കാൾ evening കൂടുതൽ ചൂടായിരുന്നു.'
• Spelling rule: hot = consonant-vowel-consonant (CVC) pattern → final consonant double: hot → hott + er = hotter ✓
• Meaning: 'രാത്രി, morning-ഇനേക്കാൾ ചൂടായിരുന്നു.'
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 ഓർക്കാനുള്ള formula: ONE-SYLLABLE adjective + 'than' → add -ER (double final consonant if CVC pattern).
• Degrees of comparison — 'hot':
  - Positive: hot (simple)
  - Comparative: hotter + than (comparing two)
  - Superlative: the hottest (comparing with all)
• CVC doubling rule (Consonant-Vowel-Consonant):
  - hot → hotter (t doubles)
  - big → bigger (g doubles)
  - thin → thinner (n doubles)
  - fat → fatter (t doubles)
  - sad → sadder (d doubles)
• 'More hot' = WRONG — 'more' used only with 2+ syllable adjectives.
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: One-syllable adjective comparative forms = PSC-ൽ regular!
• ഉദாഹരണ sentences:
  1. Today is hotter than yesterday. (hot → hotter)
  2. He is bigger than his brother. (big → bigger)
  3. This road is longer than that one. (long → longer)
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

❌ (A) hot
• 'Hot' = positive degree. 'Than' signal word ഉള്ളതിനാൽ comparative needed. 'Hot than' = wrong — positive degree + 'than' = impossible combination.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Hot' = base word, familiar. 'Hot than' natural sound feel. But 'than' = comparative marker → 'hotter' required.

❌ (C) more hot
• 'More hot' = wrong — 'more' used only with adjectives of 2+ syllables (more beautiful, more interesting). 'Hot' = 1 syllable → '-er' suffix, not 'more'.
• 'More hot' = double comparative error — never correct in English.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'More hot' = formal/emphatic feel. But 1-syllable adjectives NEVER use 'more' — always '-er'. 'More hotter' = also wrong (double comparative).

❌ (D) hottest
• 'Hottest' = superlative degree (comparing with all). 'Than' signal = comparative, not superlative.
• Superlative uses 'the' + '-est': 'the hottest day'. 'Than' + superlative = impossible.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Hottest' = extreme heat feel → fits evening context. But 'than' = two items compared → comparative (-er), not superlative (-est).
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'THAN = COMPARATIVE (-ER). THE + EST = SUPERLATIVE. Than കണ്ടാൽ -er. The + est കണ്ടാൽ superlative!' — 'Than' = two things compared = comparative. No 'than' + 'the hottest' = superlative.
• CVC doubling rule: hot→hotter, big→bigger, thin→thinner, fat→fatter. Say it: C-V-C = Double the C!
• PSC exam strategy: 'Than' ഉള്ള sentence → '-er' comparative option choose ചെയ്യുക. 'More hot' = always wrong for 1-syllable. 'Hottest' = superlative, needs 'the', not 'than'. 'Hotter' = ✓. Guaranteed mark!

questionText: Nothing will happen, __________ ?

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (A) will it?

• 'Nothing' = negative word (ഒന്നും ഇല്ല) → question tag = POSITIVE.
• Negative statement → Positive tag rule: 'Nothing will happen' (negative) → 'will it?' (positive tag) ✓
• Pronoun: 'Nothing' = thing/idea → pronoun = 'it' ✓
• Auxiliary: main sentence = 'will' → tag auxiliary = 'will' ✓
• 'Nothing will happen, will it?' = 'ഒന്നും സംഭവിക്കില്ല, അല്ലേ?'
• Meaning: Negative statement-ന് ശേഷം positive tag = confirmation/reassurance seek ചെയ്യുന്നു.
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 ഓർക്കാനുള്ള formula: NEGATIVE statement → POSITIVE tag. POSITIVE statement → NEGATIVE tag.
• 'Nothing' = negative pronoun (like no one, nobody, nowhere, never, hardly, scarcely) → sentence is NEGATIVE → tag = POSITIVE.
• Negative words that make a sentence negative:
  - nothing, nobody, no one, nowhere, never
  - hardly, scarcely, barely, seldom, rarely
  - All these → POSITIVE question tag!
• Tag construction:
  1. Identify auxiliary in main sentence: 'will'
  2. Identify pronoun for subject: 'nothing' → 'it'
  3. Sentence negative? → Positive tag: 'will it?'
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: 'Nothing/nobody/nowhere' question tags = PSC-ൽ classic trap!
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. Nothing is perfect, is it? (nothing=negative → positive tag 'is it')
  2. Nobody came, did they? (nobody=negative → positive 'did they')
  3. She never lies, does she? (never=negative → positive 'does she')
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

❌ (B) won't it?
• 'Won't it?' = negative tag. 'Nothing will happen' = already negative sentence → POSITIVE tag needed, not negative.
• 'Nothing will happen, won't it?' = negative + negative = double negative = wrong.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Will happen' positive verb feel → negative tag 'won't it' feel. But 'nothing' makes sentence negative → positive tag!

❌ (C) wouldn't it?
• 'Wouldn't' = negative of 'would' — wrong auxiliary. Main sentence = 'will', not 'would'. Tag must match main auxiliary.
• 'Would' ≠ 'will' — different tenses. Tag must use SAME auxiliary as main sentence.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Wouldn't it?' = formal/polite question feel. But wrong auxiliary (would ≠ will) AND wrong positive/negative.

❌ (D) would it?
• 'Would' = wrong auxiliary — main sentence uses 'will', not 'would'. Tag auxiliary must match.
• 'Would it?' = correct positive form BUT wrong auxiliary.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Would it?' = positive tag (correct type) but wrong auxiliary. 'Will' → 'will it?' (not 'would it?'). Auxiliary must be exact match.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'NOTHING = NEGATIVE = TAG GOES POSITIVE! Nothing negative → tag positive, like a seesaw — one side down, other side UP!' — Negative statement → positive tag. 'Nothing' = down (negative) → tag = up (positive 'will it?').
• Negative word checklist: nothing, nobody, no one, never, hardly, barely, scarcely → ALL need POSITIVE tags!
• Two-step tag formula:
  1. 'Nothing will happen' → negative sentence → positive tag
  2. Auxiliary = 'will', pronoun = 'it' → 'will it?' ✓
• PSC exam strategy: See 'nothing/nobody/never' → choose POSITIVE tag option. 'Will it?' = positive auxiliary 'will' + positive form. Guaranteed mark!

questionText:
Rewrite the following sentence using 'so … that':
The germ is too small to be seen with a naked eye.
optionA: The germ is so small that it can be seen with a naked eye.
optionB: The germ is so small that it cannot be seen with a naked eye.
optionC: The germ is so small that it could not be seen with a naked eye.
optionD: The germ is so small to be seen with a naked eye.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (B) The germ is so small that it cannot be seen with a naked eye.

• 'Too + adjective + to + verb' → 'So + adjective + that + subject + cannot + verb' = standard transformation rule ✓
• 'Too small to be seen' = 'so small that it CANNOT be seen' — negative meaning preserved ✓
• 'Too' = negative implication (ഒരു കാര്യം ചെയ്യാൻ കഴിയാത്ത അവസ്ഥ) → 'cannot' in the 'so...that' form.
• Meaning: 'ആ രോഗാണു നഗ്നനേത്രം കൊണ്ട് കാണാൻ കഴിയാത്ത വിധം ചെറുതാണ്.'
• Key: 'Too...to' = inability → 'so...that...cannot' — negative preserved always!
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 ഓർക്കാനുള്ള formula: TOO + adj + to + V1 → SO + adj + THAT + subject + CANNOT + V1
• Transformation table:
  - too small to see → so small that we cannot see
  - too heavy to lift → so heavy that we cannot lift it
  - too tired to walk → so tired that she could not walk
• ⚠️ Critical rule: 'Too' = negative meaning → 'that' clause MUST have 'cannot/could not' (NEVER 'can')
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: 'Too...to' → 'so...that' transformation = PSC-ൽ ഇടയ്ക്കിടെ!
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. He is too weak to run. → He is so weak that he cannot run.
  2. The box is too heavy to carry. → The box is so heavy that we cannot carry it.
  3. She was too busy to attend. → She was so busy that she could not attend.
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

❌ (A) ...so small that it CAN be seen...
• 'Can be seen' = positive meaning. 'Too small to be seen' = cannot be seen (negative). 'Can' ഉപയോഗിക്കുന്നത് meaning തിരിച്ചാക്കുന്നു — germ naked eye-ന് കാണാം എന്ന് ആകും, which is opposite!
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'So small that it can' = ഒഴുക്കൻ sentence feel. പക്ഷേ 'too...to' = negative → 'cannot' mandatory.

❌ (C) ...so small that it COULD NOT be seen...
• 'Could not' = past modal. Original sentence present tense ('is') → 'cannot' (present) is correct, not 'could not' (past/hypothetical).
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Could not' = formal/polite negative feel → correct sound. But tense mismatch — 'is too small' (present) → 'cannot' (present).

❌ (D) ...so small TO be seen...
• This keeps 'to' — not a proper 'so...that' transformation. 'So small to be seen' = incomplete/ungrammatical in this form.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Looks like a transformation but 'that' clause is missing. 'So...that' needs a full clause with subject + verb.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'TOO = CANNOT! Too tired to walk = so tired that I CANNOT walk. Too always means CANNOT in disguise!' — 'Too' hidden meaning = inability = cannot.
• Formula in one line: TOO + adj + to + V → SO + adj + THAT + cannot + V
• PSC exam strategy: 'Too...to' → 'so...that' question → Look for 'cannot/could not' option first. Option with 'can' (positive) = always eliminate. Option with 'to' alone (no 'that') = eliminate. 'Cannot' = ✓. Guaranteed mark!

questionText: Find the word equivalent to 'a feeling of shyness or embarrassment that stops you from doing or saying what you really want'.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (A) Inhibition

• 'Inhibition' = 'മടി, ലജ്ജ, ഉൾത്തടസ്സം' — a psychological feeling of shyness/embarrassment that holds you back.
• 'Inhibition stops you from doing/saying what you want' = exact definition ✓
• Word root: Latin 'inhibere' = to hold back (തടഞ്ഞുനിർത്തുക) → inhibition = the feeling that holds you back.
• Plural 'inhibitions': 'She has no inhibitions' = അവൾക്ക് ഒരു മടിയും ഇല്ല.
• Meaning: 'ഒരാൾ ആഗ്രഹിക്കുന്ന കാര്യം ചെയ്യുന്നതിൽ നിന്ന് തടയുന്ന ലജ്ജ/മടി/ഉൾഭയം = Inhibition.'
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 ഓർക്കാനുള്ള formula: IN- (in/against) +HIBIT (hold) = hold back feeling → INHIBITION!
• Word family of 'inhibit/inhibition':
  - inhibit (verb) = തടഞ്ഞുവയ്ക്കുക, hold back
  - inhibition (noun) = the feeling of being held back
  - inhibited (adjective) = shy, self-conscious, restrained
  - uninhibited (adjective) = free, without restraint (opposite)
• Related psychological terms (PSC vocabulary):
  - Inhibition = shyness/restraint feeling
  - Pessimism = negative outlook (ദുരാശ)
  - Optimism = positive outlook (ശുഭാപ്തിവിശ്വാസം)
  - Aggressiveness = hostile/forceful behaviour
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: One-word substitute for psychological feelings = PSC frequent!
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. Stage fright is a common inhibition. (stage-ൽ nervous = inhibition)
  2. Her inhibitions prevented her from speaking. (inhibition blocked speech)
  3. He has no inhibitions about expressing himself. (no shyness)
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

❌ (B) Miserable
• 'Miserable' = 'ദയനീയം, ദുഃഖകരം' — a feeling of being very unhappy/uncomfortable. Not specifically shyness/restraint.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Miserable' = negative feeling word → shyness = also negative feeling → seem related. But miserable = sadness/discomfort, not the specific restraint/shyness inhibition describes.

❌ (C) Pessimism
• 'Pessimism' = 'നിരാശാവാദം' — the tendency to see the worst in everything. Nothing to do with shyness.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Pessimism = negative attitude → negative feeling like shyness → related feel. But pessimism = outlook about future, not a restraining social feeling.

❌ (D) Aggressiveness
• 'Aggressiveness' = 'ആക്രമണോത്സുകത' — forceful, hostile behaviour. Complete OPPOSITE of shyness/inhibition.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Aggressiveness' = strong/intense feeling → feeling question → could fit. But inhibition = holds you BACK. Aggressiveness = pushes you FORWARD. Opposite meanings.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'INHIBITION = IN +HIBIT = held IN! Feeling held inside that stops you from coming out!' — Inhibition = trapped feeling inside, can't speak/act freely.
• 'Inhibitions' in everyday life: stage fright (stage-ൽ ഭയം), social anxiety (social ആകുമ്പോൾ nervous), shyness in public = all forms of inhibition.
• PSC exam strategy: 'Shyness/embarrassment that STOPS you' → look for 'restraint/holding back' word = Inhibition. 'Miserable' = sadness, 'Pessimism' = future outlook, 'Aggressiveness' = opposite. Only Inhibition = correct. Guaranteed mark!

questionText:
Fill in the blank in the given below sentence:
Neither of them __________ me.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (D) loves

• 'Neither of them' = singular subject — 'neither' = distributive pronoun, always singular ✓
• Singular subject → singular verb: 'loves' (not 'love') ✓
• 'Neither of them loves me' = 'അവരിൽ ആരും എന്നെ സ്നേഹിക്കുന്നില്ല.'
• 'Them' plural ആണ്, പക്ഷേ subject = 'neither' (singular) → verb = singular.
• Pattern: Neither of + plural noun/pronoun → singular verb (is/has/loves/goes etc.)
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 ഓർക്കാനുള്ള formula: NEITHER / EITHER + of + plural → SINGULAR verb!
• Distributive pronouns — always singular:
  - Neither of them LOVES (not love)
  - Either of the boys IS (not are)
  - Each of the students HAS (not have)
  - Every one of them KNOWS (not know)
• 'Love' (base form) vs 'loves' (3rd person singular):
  - He/She/It/Neither/Each/Every → loves (with 's')
  - I/We/You/They → love (no 's')
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Neither/Either + of = singular verb = PSC-ൽ ഏറ്റവും tested SVA rule!
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. Neither of the boys loves cricket. (neither → loves)
  2. Either of them is welcome. (either → is)
  3. None of the students knows the answer. (none → knows)
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

❌ (A) love
• 'Love' = plural/base form. 'Neither' = singular → 'loves' (singular) ആവശ്യം.
• 'Neither of them love me' = grammatically incorrect (plural verb with singular subject).
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Them' plural → 'love' (plural verb) feel. But subject = 'neither' (singular) → 'loves'.

❌ (B) are loving
• 'Are loving' = present continuous plural. Double error: (1) 'are' = plural auxiliary; (2) 'love' = state verb, not used in continuous form.
• 'Love' = stative verb (love, like, hate, know) — never used in '-ing' continuous form!
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Are loving' = active/continuous feel → seems dynamic. But 'love' = state (not action) → no continuous form.

❌ (C) has love
• 'Has love' = wrong verb phrase. 'Has' = auxiliary for Present Perfect (has + V3). 'Has love' is not a verb form — 'love' ≠ past participle.
• 'Loves' (simple present) = correct; 'has loved' (present perfect) = possible; but 'has love' = neither.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Has' = singular feel → correct for 'neither'. But 'has + love' (noun) = not a valid verb structure.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'NEITHER = ONE of two things — ONE is singular! Neither of them LOVES (like He loves)!' — Neither = picking one from two = singular. He loves, She loves, Neither loves!
• State verbs — NEVER in continuous (important for PSC):
  - love, like, hate, know, believe, understand, want, need → never 'is loving, are loving'
  - Always: loves, knows, believes (simple present)
• PSC exam strategy: 'Neither of them + blank' → choose singular verb with 's' = 'loves'. 'love' (plural) = eliminate. 'are loving' (continuous state verb) = eliminate. 'has love' (wrong structure) = eliminate. 'loves' = ✓. Guaranteed mark!

questionText:
Change into indirect speech:
"Alas! My uncle met with an accident", he said.
optionA: My uncle Alas had met with an accident
optionB: He cried out that his uncle had met with an accident
optionC: He told his uncle that he had met with an accident
optionD: He cried that his uncle Alas met with an accident

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (B) He cried out that his uncle had met with an accident

• 'Alas!' = exclamation of grief/sorrow → indirect speech reporting verb = 'cried out' ✓
• 'My' → 'his' (pronoun change: first person → third person) ✓
• 'Met' (Simple Past) → 'had met' (Past Perfect — backshift) ✓
• 'Alas' = interjection — NOT included in indirect speech (only meaning/emotion preserved through 'cried out') ✓
• Meaning: 'അവൻ വിലപിച്ചു, തന്റെ അമ്മാവന് അപകടം പറ്റിയെന്ന്.'
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 ഓർക്കാനുള്ള formula: Exclamatory sentences → indirect speech: said → cried out/exclaimed. Interjection removed. Tense backshift.
• Exclamatory indirect speech rules:
  1. Reporting verb: 'said' → 'cried out' / 'exclaimed' / 'shouted' (based on emotion)
  2. Interjections (Alas!, Hurrah!, Oh!, Wow!) → removed from indirect speech
  3. Pronoun change: 'My' → 'his/her' (based on context)
  4. Tense backshift: Simple Past ('met') → Past Perfect ('had met')
• Exclamation types and reporting verbs:
  - Alas! (grief) → cried out, exclaimed sorrowfully
  - Hurrah! (joy) → cried out, exclaimed joyfully
  - Oh! / Wow! → exclaimed
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Exclamatory sentences → indirect speech = PSC-ൽ regular!
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. 'Alas! I have lost my purse' → She cried out that she had lost her purse.
  2. 'Hurrah! We won!' → They exclaimed joyfully that they had won.
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

❌ (A) My uncle Alas had met with an accident
• 'Alas' = interjection — indirect speech-ൽ 'Alas' word include ചെയ്യരുത്. Plus no reporting verb/subject ('he said that' missing entirely).
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Alas' word keep ചെയ്‌ത് tense backshift ('had met') = partial correct feel. But interjection must be removed, reporting verb must be added.

❌ (C) He told his uncle that he had met with an accident
• 'Told his uncle' = wrong — the sentence says 'My uncle met an accident' (uncle is the victim), not that he told his uncle something.
• Meaning distorted: (C) implies he spoke TO his uncle. But original = he spoke ABOUT his uncle.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Told his uncle' = smooth sentence. But 'told his uncle' = uncle ആണ് listener. Original = uncle = accident victim. Meaning completely changed!

❌ (D) He cried that his uncle Alas met with an accident
• Two errors: (1) 'Alas' still included (interjection must be removed); (2) 'met' = no tense backshift (should be 'had met').
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Cried' as reporting verb = correct direction. But 'Alas' kept + no backshift = incomplete transformation.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'ALAS = SADNESS → CRIED OUT! Indirect speech = throw away Alas, use cried out instead!' — Interjection (Alas/Hurrah) = trash can (throwaway) in indirect speech. Replace with emotional reporting verb.
• 3-step exclamatory transformation:
  1. Remove interjection (Alas → gone)
  2. Change reporting verb (said → cried out)
  3. Backshift tense (met → had met) + pronoun (my → his)
• PSC exam strategy: 'Alas!' indirect speech → option with 'cried out' + no 'Alas' + tense backshift = correct. Eliminate options keeping 'Alas' word or wrong meaning. (B) = only correct. Guaranteed mark!

questionText:
Change the following sentence into superlative degree:
Light travels faster than anything else.
optionA: Anything else travels faster than light in the world.
optionB: Anything else travels fastest than light in the world.
optionC: Of all things in the world, light travels the fastest.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (C) Of all things in the world, light travels the fastest.

• 'Faster than anything else' (comparative) → 'the fastest of all things' (superlative) ✓
• Superlative form: fast → faster → the fastest ✓
• 'Of all things in the world' = superlative context (comparing with EVERYTHING) ✓
• Structure: 'Of all + noun, subject + verb + the + superlative' ✓
• Meaning: 'ലോകത്തിലെ എല്ലാ കാര്യങ്ങളിലും, വെളിച്ചം ഏറ്റവും വേഗത്തിൽ സഞ്ചരിക്കുന്നു.'
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 ഓർക്കാനുള്ള formula: Comparative 'faster than anything else' → Superlative 'the fastest of all'
• Degree transformation table for 'fast':
  - Positive: Light travels fast.
  - Comparative: Light travels faster than anything else.
  - Superlative: Of all things, light travels the fastest. ✓
• Superlative structure patterns:
  - 'Of all + noun, subject + the + superlative'
  - 'Subject + the + superlative + of all'
  - 'Subject + the + superlative + noun'
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Comparative → Superlative transformation = PSC-ൽ class question!
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. She is taller than anyone else → She is the tallest of all.
  2. He runs faster than all others → He runs the fastest of all.
  3. Gold is more expensive than any other metal → Gold is the most expensive metal.
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

❌ (A) Anything else travels faster than light in the world.
• This reverses the meaning — 'anything else' travels faster than light = wrong! Light is the fastest, not 'anything else'.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Sentence restructured with 'anything else' as subject = sounds like a transformation. But meaning flipped — light is fastest, not other things.

❌ (B) Anything else travels fastest than light in the world.
• Double error: (1) meaning reversed (same as A); (2) 'fastest than' = wrong — superlative never uses 'than' ('the fastest OF all' / 'faster THAN' — never mix).
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Fastest' = superlative word = correct degree. But 'fastest than' = impossible combination + meaning reversed.

❌ (D) Nothing is faster than light.
• This is a different form (positive degree using negative: 'nothing is faster than') — not a superlative transformation.
• 'Nothing is faster' = valid expression of the idea but NOT the superlative degree form requested.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Nothing is faster than light' = logically correct and familiar. But question asks for SUPERLATIVE form specifically → 'the fastest' structure needed.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'Faster than anything else → THE FASTEST of all! Remove anything else, add THE FASTEST and OF ALL!' — 'Anything else' comparison = comparative. 'Of all things' = superlative. Swap them!
• Transformation shortcut: 'faster than anything/anyone else' → 'the fastest of all'
• PSC exam strategy: Comparative → superlative question → find option with 'the + adjective-est + of all' structure. Subject must remain light (fastest traveller). (C) = 'light travels the fastest' = correct subject + superlative. Guaranteed mark!

questionText:
Choose the correct preposition to fill in the blank given below:
The burglar jumped __________ the compound wall.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (D) over

• 'Jump over' = ഒരു obstacle കടന്ന്/ചാടിക്കടന്ന് അപ്പുറം പോകുക — wall-ന്റെ മറുഭാഗത്ത് എത്തുക ✓
• 'The burglar jumped over the compound wall' = 'കള്ളൻ compound wall ചാടിക്കടന്നു.' ✓
• 'Over' = from one side to the other side, clearing the obstacle.
• Physical action: burglar was on one side → jumped → landed on the other side = 'over' perfectly describes this motion.
• Meaning: 'കള്ളൻ compound wall-ന് മുകളിലൂടെ ചാടി അതിർ കടന്നു.'
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 ഓർക്കാനുള്ള formula: JUMP OVER = ഒരു obstacle കടന്നുപോകുക (clear from one side to other)
• Movement prepositions with 'jump':
  - jump OVER = ചാടിക്കടക്കുക (wall/fence/hurdle — clear an obstacle)
  - jump INTO = ചാടി അകത്ത് കയറുക (jump into a pool)
  - jump OUT OF = ചാടി പുറത്ത് വരുക (jump out of a window)
  - jump AT = eagerly accept (jump at a chance = അവസരം ആർത്തിയോടെ സ്വീകരിക്കുക — idiomatic)
  - jump ONTO = ചാടി കയറുക (jump onto the bus)
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Movement prepositions (over/into/onto/out of) = PSC-ൽ regular!
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. The horse jumped over the fence. (fence കടന്ന് ചാടി)
  2. The child jumped into the water. (വെള്ളത്തിലേക്ക് ചാടി)
  3. She jumped over the puddle. (water puddle കടന്ന് ചാടി)
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

❌ (A) at
• 'Jump at' = idiomatic meaning — eagerly accept an opportunity ('jump at the chance'). Physical wall context-ൽ 'jump at a wall' = wrong meaning.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'At' = location preposition feel → wall location → 'at the wall' feel. But 'jump at' = idiom (eagerness), not physical action.

❌ (B) in
• 'Jump in' = enter something ('jump in the pool'). Wall = vertical barrier, not a container to enter. 'Jumped in the wall' = physically impossible meaning.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'In' = very common preposition → familiar choice. But 'in' = inside/enclosed space, not crossing a barrier.

❌ (C) by
• 'Jump by' = not a standard preposition phrase with 'jump'. 'By' = beside/method ('standing by the wall' ✓, but 'jump by' = no standard meaning).
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'By the wall' = standing next to it feel. But 'jumped by the wall' = unclear/wrong meaning for crossing action.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'OVER = going ABOVE and BEYOND! Jump OVER = fly above the wall and land on the other side!' — Imagine the burglar jumping UP and OVER the wall like an arch — OVER!
• Preposition picture: at = pointing to, in = inside, by = beside, over = above and crossing.
• PSC exam strategy: 'Jumped + wall/fence/hurdle' → always 'over' (crossing obstacle). 'At' = eagerness idiom. 'In' = entering. 'By' = beside. Physical crossing = OVER. Guaranteed mark!

questionText: Find out the meaning of the idiom 'tie yourself up in knots'.
optionB: make no progress in an argument
optionC: become very confused when you are trying to explain something

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (C) become very confused when you are trying to explain something

• 'Tie yourself up in knots' = 'കുരുക്കിൽ കുടുങ്ങുക' — confuse yourself so much that you can't explain clearly.
• Visual image: rope-ൽ knots (കുരുക്കുകൾ) ഉണ്ടാക്കിയാൽ rope unravel ആകില്ല — similarly, thoughts knot ആകുമ്പോൾ explanation unravel ആകില്ല.
• 'Tie up in knots' = thoughts/words കുരുങ്ങി, explanation difficult ആകുന്ന സ്ഥിതി ✓
• Meaning: 'ഒരു കാര്യം explain ചെയ്യാൻ ശ്രമിക്കുന്തോറും കൂടുതൽ confused ആകുന്ന അവസ്ഥ.'
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 ഓർക്കാനുള്ള formula: KNOTS = CONFUSION! Tie in knots = tangled thoughts = confused explanation!
• Idioms with 'knot/tie' theme:
  - tie yourself up in knots = become confused explaining
  - tie the knot = marry (വിവാഹം കഴിക്കുക)
  - at a rate of knots = very fast (വളരെ വേഗത്തിൽ)
  - a knotty problem = a very difficult problem (കുഴഞ്ഞ problem)
• Related 'confusion' idioms for PSC:
  - in a muddle = confused
  - at sea = confused/lost
  - go around in circles = make no progress
  - lose the thread = forget what you were saying
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Idiom meanings = PSC-ൽ ഇടയ്ക്കിടെ!
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. He tied himself up in knots trying to explain the rule. (explain ചെയ്യതോ confused)
  2. She gets tied up in knots whenever she speaks in public. (public speech = confused)
  3. Stop tying yourself up in knots — just say it simply! (advice to be clear)
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

❌ (A) believes something good about someone
• 'Believes something good' = positive belief about a person. 'Tie in knots' = confusion — nothing to do with belief or positive opinion.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Knots' = tight/strong → strongly believe feel. But 'tie in knots' = tangled confusion, not firm belief.

❌ (B) make no progress in an argument
• 'Make no progress' = stuck/stagnant. 'Tie in knots' specifically = confusion during EXPLANATION, not just lack of progress.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Being confused → could mean making no progress. But the idiom specifically = confusion during explaining, not general stagnation.

❌ (D) trying to modify an opinion
• 'Modify an opinion' = change one's view. No connection to 'tying knots' imagery.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Knots' = changing/adjusting feel? No logical connection. The image is tangled confusion, not opinion modification.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'Tie in KNOTS = your THOUGHTS get KNOTTED like tangled earphones! Can't straighten them out to explain clearly!' — Earphones/headphones കുരുങ്ങിയ image → thoughts confused → explanation confused = tied up in knots!
• PSC idiom tip: Idiom meaning questions → think about the visual image. Knots = tangled = confused. Which option matches 'confused while explaining'? = (C).
• PSC exam strategy: 'Tie in knots' → look for 'confused/confusing' option. Eliminate 'believe' (no connection), 'no progress' (partial), 'modify opinion' (no connection). (C) = confused explanation = exact match. Guaranteed mark!

questionText:
Complete the following sentence using suitable articles:
On __________ way we saw __________ __one-eyed__ beggar.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (B) the, a

• Blank 1: 'On THE way' = ഒരു specific journey/route refer ചെയ്യുന്നു — definite article 'the' ✓
• Blank 2: 'A one-eyed beggar' — 'one' word-ന്റെ ഉച്ചാരണം = /wʌn/ = 'w' consonant sound → 'a' (not 'an') ✓
• 'On the way' = fixed phrase — the journey (specific) → 'the'.
• 'A one-eyed beggar' = first mention, indefinite + 'one' = /w/ sound → 'a'.
• Meaning: 'യാത്രയ്ക്കിടയിൽ ഞങ്ങൾ ഒരു ഒറ്റക്കണ്ണൻ യാചകനെ കണ്ടു.'
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 ഓർക്കാനുള്ള formula: THE = specific/known. A/AN = first mention/indefinite. A/AN = SOUND-based (not spelling)!
• Blank 1 — 'the way':
  - 'On the way' = fixed expression for a specific journey ✓
  - 'On a way' = wrong (no such fixed expression)
• Blank 2 — 'one-eyed':
  - 'One' = pronounced /wʌn/ — starts with /w/ = consonant sound → 'A'
  - NOT 'an one' — even though 'o' is a vowel letter, 'one' = /w/ consonant sound!
• Tricky words starting with vowel letter but consonant sound → 'A':
  - a one-eyed (wʌn = /w/)
  - a university (ju: = /j/)
  - a unique (ju: = /j/)
  - a European (jʊər = /j/)
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: 'One-eyed / one + noun' article = PSC-ൽ classic trick!
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. On the way, we stopped for tea. (the way = specific journey)
  2. She met a one-armed soldier. (one = /w/ sound → a)
  3. It was a one-time offer. (one = /w/ → a)
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

❌ (A) a, an
• 'A way' = wrong ('on the way' = fixed expression needing 'the'). 'An one-eyed' = wrong ('one' = /w/ sound → 'a'). Double error!
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'An one-eyed' = 'one' starts with 'O' vowel letter → 'an' feel. But SOUND = /w/ consonant → 'a'.

❌ (C) the, an
• 'The way' ✓ (correct!), but 'an one-eyed' ✗ ('one' = /w/ consonant sound → 'a', not 'an').
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Most common mistake — 'the' correct for blank 1, BUT 'an one' = wrong. 'One' = /wʌn/ → 'a'. This is the most tempting wrong answer!

❌ (D) a, a
• 'A way' = wrong (should be 'the way' — specific journey). 'A one-eyed' = correct (a one = /w/ sound). One correct, one wrong.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'A one-eyed' = correct! But 'a way' = wrong. 'On the way' is fixed phrase requiring 'the'.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'ONE sounds like WON (won a race) — W sound! So it's A one, not AN one! A one-eyed, a one-time, a one-way!' — 'One' = say it aloud: /wʌn/ = starts with W → A!
• Two-blank article strategy:
  1. 'On ___ way' → always 'the way' (fixed phrase) → eliminate A and D
  2. '___ one-eyed' → 'one' = /w/ sound → 'a' → eliminate C (an)
  3. Only (B) the, a = both correct!
• PSC exam strategy: Two blanks → eliminate wrong options step by step. 'The way' = fixed phrase → eliminates A(a,an) and D(a,a). 'One' = /w/ sound → 'a' → eliminates C(the,an). (B) the, a = ✓. Guaranteed mark!

questionText:
What is the expansion and meaning of the abbreviation given below?
AM

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (B) Ante-Meridiem, Before Midday

• 'AM' = Ante Meridiem (Latin) = 'Meridiem-ന് മുമ്പ്' = Before Midday (ഉച്ചക്ക് മുമ്പ്) ✓
• Latin 'ante' = before (മുമ്പ്); 'meridiem' = midday/noon (ഉച്ചനേരം).
• AM = 12:00 midnight to 12:00 noon (midnight മുതൽ ഉച്ചവരെ).
• PM = Post Meridiem = After Midday (ഉച്ചക്ക് ശേഷം = 12:00 noon to midnight).
• Meaning: '9 AM = 9 o'clock in the morning = രാവിലെ 9 മണി.'
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 ഓർക്കാനുള്ള formula: AM = ANTE (before) + MERIDIEM (midday) = BEFORE MIDDAY. PM = POST (after) + MERIDIEM = AFTER MIDDAY!
• Latin roots (PSC must-know):
  - Ante = before (മുമ്പ്) → AM = before noon
  - Post = after (ശേഷം) → PM = after noon
  - Meridiem = midday/noon (ഉച്ചനേരം)
• Time abbreviations for PSC:
  - AM = Ante Meridiem = Before Midday (12 midnight – 12 noon)
  - PM = Post Meridiem = After Midday (12 noon – 12 midnight)
  - GMT = Greenwich Mean Time
  - IST = Indian Standard Time
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Common abbreviations (AM/PM/GMT etc.) = PSC-ൽ regular!
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. The exam starts at 10 AM. (10 in the morning)
  2. The meeting is at 3 PM. (3 in the afternoon)
  3. 12 AM = midnight; 12 PM = noon (ഇവ confuse ആകാൻ സാധ്യത)
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

❌ (A) After Midday, After Noon
• 'After Midday' = PM-ന്റെ meaning! AM = BEFORE midday, PM = AFTER midday. Complete confusion!
• 🪤 PSC Trap: AM/PM confuse ആകുന്നത് common. 'After Midday' = PM meaning. AM = Ante (before) = morning. Never 'After' with AM.

❌ (C) Ante-Meridiem, After Noon
• Expansion correct: 'Ante-Meridiem' ✓. But meaning wrong: 'After Noon' = PM-ന്റെ meaning. Ante = before, not after!
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Ante-Meridiem' = looks impressive/correct → choose this. But meaning 'After Noon' = contradicts 'ante' (before). Half right = fully wrong!

❌ (D) After Midday, Morning
• Both wrong: expansion wrong (After Midday ≠ Ante Meridiem) AND meaning partially confused.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Morning' = AM = morning time = correct feeling. But AM ≠ 'After Midday' expansion. 'Morning' alone doesn't fully define AM (AM = midnight to noon, not just morning).
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'A in AM = ANTE = AADHYAM (ആദ്യം)! A comes FIRST in alphabet = AM comes FIRST in the day (morning)! P in PM = POST = PINNALE (പിന്നാലെ)! P comes after A = PM comes after AM!' — A before P in alphabet = AM before PM in the day!
• Simple memory: AM = Morning time (after midnight, before noon). PM = Evening/Night (after noon, before midnight).
• PSC exam strategy: 'AM' expansion = Ante-Meridiem (not After). Meaning = Before Midday (not After). Only (B) has both correct: Ante-Meridiem + Before Midday. Guaranteed mark!

questionText: Find out the Synonym for the word 'Feud'.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (C) Quarrel

• 'Feud' = 'ദീർഘകാല വൈരം, കുടുംബ ശത്രുത, bitter conflict' — a long-lasting angry dispute, especially between families/groups.
• 'Quarrel' = 'വഴക്ക്, തർക്കം, conflict' = synonym of feud ✓ (both = angry dispute)
• 'Feud' = usually longer/more bitter than a simple quarrel — family feuds can last generations.
• Word root: Old French 'faide' = hostility (ശത്രുത).
• Meaning: 'Feud = ദീർഘകാലമായ കടുത്ത വൈരം/വഴക്ക്, especially between families.'
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 ഓർക്കാനുള്ള formula: FEUD = long-lasting QUARREL/CONFLICT — family/group enmity over time!
• Word family of 'feud':
  - feud (noun) = long bitter dispute (ദീർഘകാല ശത്രുത)
  - feudal (adjective) = relating to feudalism (feudal system)
  - feuding (verb/adjective) = engaged in a feud (feuding families)
• Synonyms of feud: quarrel, conflict, dispute, enmity, rivalry, vendetta, hostility
• Antonyms of feud: friendship, harmony, peace, accord, goodwill
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Vocabulary synonyms with misleading options = PSC classic!
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. The two families had a feud for decades. (decades = long quarrel)
  2. Their feud over land finally ended. (property dispute = feud)
  3. The celebrity feud made news. (public conflict = feud)
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

❌ (A) Friendship
• 'Friendship' = 'സൗഹൃദം' — exact ANTONYM of feud. Feud = enmity; friendship = opposite.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Feud' = sounds like a relationship word (like 'friend' pattern?) → 'friendship' feel. But 'feud' = hostility, 'friendship' = harmony. Completely opposite!

❌ (B) Father-in-law
• 'Father-in-law' = 'അമ്മായിയപ്പൻ' — a family relationship, not an English vocabulary synonym. Completely unrelated.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Feud' sometimes associated with families ('family feud') → family relation 'father-in-law' = feel related. But feud = quarrel/conflict, not a family member title.

❌ (D) Ignorant
• 'Ignorant' = 'അജ്ഞൻ, അറിവില്ലാത്ത' — completely unrelated to feud. Feud = conflict, ignorant = lack of knowledge.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Ignorant' = negative word → feud also negative → both negative = seem synonymous. But negative ≠ same meaning. Conflict ≠ ignorance.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'FEUD = FOOD FIGHT! Family FEUD = long family food/quarrel fight! FEUD rhymes with RUDE — rude people quarrel!' — Family Feud (TV show!) = famous family quarrel show → FEUD = quarrel/conflict!
• Quick synonym pairs to remember:
  - Feud = Quarrel/Conflict/Vendetta
  - Amity = Friendship (amity = feud-ന്റെ antonym)
  - Enmity = Hostility/Hatred
• PSC exam strategy: 'Feud' synonym → eliminate opposites first. 'Friendship' (A) = antonym → eliminate. 'Father-in-law' (B) = unrelated person → eliminate. 'Ignorant' (D) = different category → eliminate. 'Quarrel' (C) = conflict = ✓. Guaranteed mark!

questionText:
Choose the correct question tag.
He has left his home, __________

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (D) hasn't he?

• 'He has left his home' = Positive statement → Negative question tag ✓
• Auxiliary verb: 'has' → tag auxiliary = 'hasn't' (negative of 'has') ✓
• Pronoun: 'He' → tag pronoun = 'he' ✓
• Positive statement → Negative tag rule: 'has' → 'hasn't he?' ✓
• 'He has left his home, hasn't he?' = 'അദ്ദേഹം വീട് വിട്ടുപോയി, അല്ലേ?'
• Meaning: Positive sentence-ന് ശേഷം confirmation ചോദിക്കുന്ന negative tag.
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 ഓർക്കാനുള്ള formula: POSITIVE statement → NEGATIVE tag. NEGATIVE statement → POSITIVE tag.
• Question tag construction steps:
  1. Identify the auxiliary: 'has' (present perfect)
  2. Make it negative: 'hasn't'
  3. Identify the subject pronoun: 'he'
  4. Tag = 'hasn't he?'
• Present Perfect question tags:
  - He has gone → hasn't he?
  - She has finished → hasn't she?
  - They have arrived → haven't they?
  - We have tried → haven't we?
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Question tags with has/have = PSC-ൽ classic!
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. She has eaten, hasn't she? (has → hasn't)
  2. They have left, haven't they? (have → haven't)
  3. He has won, hasn't he? (has → hasn't)
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

❌ (A) have he?
• 'Have he' = wrong — two errors: (1) 'have' = plural form; subject 'he' = singular → 'has/hasn't'; (2) positive tag for positive statement = wrong.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Have' = familiar word, 'he' = correct pronoun → looks plausible. But 'he' = singular → 'has/hasn't', not 'have'. Plus positive sentence → negative tag.

❌ (B) haven't he?
• 'Haven't he' = wrong auxiliary. 'He' = singular → needs 'hasn't' (not 'haven't'). 'Haven't' = used with 'they/we/you/I'.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Negative tag ✓ (correct direction), 'he' pronoun ✓. But 'haven't he' = wrong — singular 'he' needs 'hasn't'. 'Haven't they' ✓ but 'haven't he' ✗.

❌ (C) is he?
• 'Is he?' = wrong auxiliary entirely. Main verb = 'has left' (Present Perfect with 'has'). Tag must use SAME auxiliary → 'hasn't he?', not 'is he?'.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Is he?' = familiar, simple question tag feel. But auxiliary mismatch — 'has' ≠ 'is'. Tag must mirror the main auxiliary.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'HE HAS → HE HASN'T! Positive to Negative, same verb, same pronoun — just add NOT!' — He has (positive) → hasn't he (negative tag). Mirror the verb, flip positive/negative!
• Singular vs Plural with have:
  - He/She/It + HAS → HASN'T he/she/it?
  - I/We/You/They + HAVE → HAVEN'T I/we/you/they?
• PSC exam strategy: 'He has left' → Step 1: positive → negative tag. Step 2: auxiliary = 'has' → 'hasn't'. Step 3: pronoun = 'he'. Answer = 'hasn't he?' = (D). Guaranteed mark!

questionText:
Choose the correct one word substitute for the underlined words in the given sentence.
She teaches the art of producing __beautiful handwriting__.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (A) Calligraphy

• 'Calligraphy' = 'സുലേഖനം, beautiful handwriting-ന്റെ കല' — the art of producing beautiful, decorative handwriting ✓
• Word root: Greek 'kallos' = beauty (സൗന്ദര്യം) + 'graphia' = writing (എഴുത്ത്) → beautiful writing = Calligraphy ✓
• 'She teaches calligraphy' = 'അവൾ സുലേഖന കല പഠിപ്പിക്കുന്നു.'
• Calligraphers use special pens/brushes to create artistic, decorative text.
• Meaning: 'Calligraphy = മനോഹരമായ കൈയ്യക്ഷര കല — artistic handwriting.'
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 ഓർക്കാനുള്ള formula: CALLI (beautiful) + GRAPHY (writing) = CALLIGRAPHY = beautiful writing!
• '-graphy' suffix = writing/recording (PSC must-know):
  - Calligraphy = beautiful handwriting (calli = beauty)
  - Choreography = art of dance composition (choreo = dance)
  - Demography = study of human populations (demo = people)
  - Photography = writing with light (photo = light)
  - Biography = life writing (bio = life)
  - Geography = earth writing (geo = earth)
  - Stenography = shorthand writing (steno = narrow)
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: '-graphy' word meanings = PSC-ൽ frequent!
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. Islamic calligraphy is considered an art form. (Arabic beautiful writing)
  2. The calligrapher wrote the wedding invitations. (handwriting artist)
  3. She learned calligraphy to improve her handwriting. (beautiful writing art)
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

❌ (B) Choreography
• 'Choreography' = 'നൃത്ത സംവിധാനം' — the art of composing/arranging dance sequences. Nothing to do with handwriting.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Both 'calligraphy' and 'choreography' end in '-graphy' → seem similar. But 'choreo' = dance, 'calli' = beauty/writing. Dance ≠ handwriting.

❌ (C) Demography
• 'Demography' = 'ജനസംഖ്യാ ശാസ്ത്രം' — the study of human populations (birth rate, death rate, etc.). Unrelated to handwriting.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Demography' also ends in '-graphy' → scientific study feel. But 'demo' = people/population, not writing art.

❌ (D) None of the above
• (A) Calligraphy = exact correct answer. 'None of the above' is wrong here.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: When unsure, 'None of the above' feels safe. But Calligraphy = precisely defined as 'the art of beautiful handwriting'. Never choose 'none' when a perfect match exists.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'CALLI sounds like KALLI (കള്ളി — sharp/beautiful in feel) + GRAPHY = WRITING. BEAUTIFUL WRITING = CALLIGRAPHY!' — 'Calli' = beautiful. Graphy = writing. Calli + graphy = beautiful writing!
• '-graphy' family cheat sheet:
  - CALLI + graphy = Beautiful writing 🖊️
  - CHOREO + graphy = Dance composition 💃
  - DEMO + graphy = Population study 👥
  - PHOTO + graphy = Light/pictures 📷
  - BIO + graphy = Life story 📖
• PSC exam strategy: 'Art of beautiful handwriting' → 'Calli' (beautiful) + 'graphy' (writing) = Calligraphy. Eliminate dance (choreo) and population (demo). Guaranteed mark!

questionText: What is the plural of 'Daughter-in-law'?

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (B) Daughters in-law

• Compound noun 'Daughter-in-law' → plural = main noun 'daughter' gets the plural → 'Daughters-in-law' ✓
• Rule: Compound nouns-ൽ MAIN NOUN pluralize ചെയ്യുക, not the last word.
• 'Daughter' = main noun in 'daughter-in-law' → 'Daughters-in-law' ✓
• 'In-law' = describes the relationship (not the main noun) → unchanged.
• Meaning: 'Daughter-in-law-ന്റെ plural = Daughters-in-law (മരുമക്കൾ — ഒന്നിലധികം).'
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 ഓർക്കാനുള്ള formula: Compound nouns → MAIN NOUN gets plural (usually first or most important noun)
• '-in-law' compound noun plurals:
  - Daughter-in-law → Daughters-in-law ✓
